4.9%

Uganda

2.7%

Uruguay

2.1%

Uzbekistan

0.9%

Zambia

2.5%

All of these countries are generally considered to be “emerging markets,” although admittedly, the definition of that term can be somewhat vague. The Corporate Finance Institute offers the following definition:

Countries that are transitioning from the “developing” phase to the “developed” phase.

This definition admittedly is not particularly helpful, especially because it is not exactly clear what makes a country “developed.” WhyEduFinance states that there is no agreed-upon definition of an “emerging market,” and as such major financial institutions and fund managers usually just make up their own definitions based on certain characteristics of emerging markets. In particular, emerging markets usually have much stronger gross domestic product growth than developed markets, along with economies that are moving away from farming and towards manufacturing and technology development. However, even this definition is not precise, as MSCI still classifies South Korea as an emerging market despite the fact that many of the technological products used in the United States and the European Union are designed and manufactured there. Thus, it can be a matter of opinion which specific countries are considered to be emerging markets and, as such, which countries might be represented in this fund. However, potential investors can likely be certain that the United States, Canada, Japan, Australia, and most nations in the European Union will not have their debt securities included in this fund.

One thing that anyone reviewing the above list will likely notice is that the Templeton Emerging Markets Income Fund is pretty well-diversified across countries. There are only six countries—Ecuador, India, Ivory Coast, Kazakhstan, Rwanda, and South Africa—that account for more than 5% of the fund’s net assets. If we include the Egyptian Treasury bills, then that country also accounts for more than 5% of the fund’s net assets, but as we can see here, those securities will all mature before the end of the year:

The most long-dated of the Egyptian Treasury bills matures in October, so the fund should not have its money tied up for too long in those securities. This is a nice characteristic for investors, as generally speaking, the closer to the present a debt security’s maturity date, the less likely it is to default. After all, there are fewer things that could potentially occur within the next few months than there are things that could potentially occur within the next ten years. As such, the default risk on these securities is probably not too high, but it may still be a good idea to be cautious. As the Harvard Kennedy School points out, Egypt has suffered eight balance of payment crises since 1952, with the most recent occurring in 2023 and 2024. Ultimately, the International Monetary Fund had to bail out the nation in early 2024. Today, the country is trying to return its economy to growth and reduce its external sovereign debt. The country has been seeing an increase in foreign investment lately, and naturally, it wants this trend to continue, as that is critical to the country being able to execute its economic development plan. The last thing that the government would want is to lose the confidence of foreign investors, and as such, it seems likely that the Egyptian government will do everything in its power to avoid defaulting on its short-term Treasury bills. As such, we can probably expect that the securities above will all be paid as agreed, and given the interest rate on them, the fund might stand to earn a very attractive profit as the bills mature.

One thing that we should note, however, is that the Egyptian Treasury bills are all denominated in Egyptian pounds. This means that the interest and face value to be paid to the fund at maturity will be in the form of that currency. This exposes the fund to currency risk, which could be problematic. As this chart shows, the Egyptian pound is down 8.20% against the U.S. dollar year-to-date:

This is not what we want to see when considering foreign currency-denominated bonds for purchase. This is because a decline in the EGP/USD exchange rate means that the Egyptian pounds that the fund receives at the maturity of each of the Treasury bills will convert into fewer U.S. dollars than was the case two months ago. In other words, the Egyptian Treasury bills are losing value in U.S. dollar terms. Obviously, this is not a good thing for investors in this fund. The fact that most of the Egyptian Treasury bills are paying 23% or higher interest rates should help to offset the losses from the currency exchange fluctuations, but it might still be a good idea to keep an eye on the exchange rate if you happen to be invested in this fund.

Al-Estiklal offers three explanations for the year-to-date decline in the Egyptian pound against the U.S. dollar. They mostly boil down to international investors attempting to reduce their exposure to what are perceived to be “risky assets,” particularly in the Middle East. It is very possible that the outbreak of hostilities between the United States, Israel, and Iran in the region has made investors nervous about having exposure to the area. While Egypt itself is not involved in this conflict, recent events have still made many participants in the international markets nervous, and, as such, they have begun moving their money out of emerging markets and back to developed markets. This trend is also evident in recent U.S. Treasury auctions, which have been showing signs of increasing appetites among international investors. For example, Zero Hedge points out that international buyers were fairly active in today’s ten-year U.S. Treasury auction, especially when compared to recent ones. We have also seen this in the stock market, which experienced a sell-off once the war broke out, as investors switched from risk assets (common equities) to cash. Currency valuations are highly affected by capital flows. As investors pull their money out of a country, they also sell the currency of that nation in order to convert it into the currency of a nation that they deem to be safer. That causes the currency of the country whose currency is being sold to decline against currencies such as the U.S. dollar. Unfortunately for the Templeton Emerging Markets Income Fund, it is difficult to see a situation where the Egyptian pound returns to its previous value before the hostilities in the Middle East end. There are a range of opinions on when that could be, but I would not be surprised if at least some of the fund’s Egyptian Treasury bills mature before investors become more confident about investing in Egypt and its currency regains the value that it had at the start of the year.

It is very possible that this weakness in the Egyptian currency has been a contributing factor in the fund’s poor performance since the start of this year:

We can see that the fund’s share price is down 0.62% since the start of 2026. However, it was actually up on the year in mid-February. Over the period from the start of the year through February 12, 2026, the fund’s share price was up 8.37%. As we can see here, it has fallen by 8.73% since that date:

This roughly corresponds to the same period during which the Egyptian currency was declining.

Admittedly, the decline of the Egyptian pound against the U.S. dollar is not the only reason for the fund’s share price decline. After all, only 12.93% of the fund’s assets are denominated in Egyptian pounds:

There are other currencies that are more highly represented in this fund, including the South African rand at 15.40% of total assets. In addition, the fund has 12.39% of its assets invested in Indian rupee-denominated bonds and 11.63% of its assets in bonds that are denominated in Brazilian reals. Thus, the fund does not really have excessive exposure to any foreign currency. In fact, its largest position is a negative 18.99% position in the U.S. dollar:

This could hurt the fund in a flight-to-safety situation, given that the U.S. dollar is the usual beneficiary in such situations. Basically, whenever uncertainty rattles the markets, investors tend to sell common stocks, emerging market bonds, emerging market currencies, and certain commodities. They use the proceeds of these sales to purchase U.S. Treasuries, U.S. dollars, and sometimes gold. This process tends to cause the U.S. dollar to rise against other currencies. In the case of this fund, that would be a negative, as the value of the fund’s long positions will decline when measured in U.S. dollars. It is likely that this is the biggest reason for the fund’s price decline over the past few weeks. The decline in the Egyptian pound to U.S. dollar exchange rate was not the sole cause.

It is important to watch currency markets with a fund like this due to the fact that bonds can be thought of as a promise to deliver a certain amount of a specified currency at a future date. An investor purchases a bond from the issuer at face value (or a discount to face value, in the case of a zero-coupon bond). The issuer then delivers a specified amount of fiat currency to the owner of the bond on a specified schedule and then pays the face amount of currency to the bondholder on the date that the bond matures. The actual value of these payments varies depending on the value of the currency. With a bond that is denominated in a currency other than the investor’s own, the payments (and the bond) increase in value when the currency in which the bond is denominated rises against the investor’s own currency and vice versa. As of the time of writing, all of the long positions of the Templeton Emerging Markets Income Fund are denominated in a currency other than the U.S. dollar. As such, investors in this fund would be wise to pay at least some attention to the foreign exchange market, as the movements that occur there will have a major impact on the value of the fund’s assets.

Fortunately, the Templeton Emerging Markets Income Fund is diversified enough that it is unlikely that a movement in the value of a single currency will have an outsized impact on its portfolio. The sole exception here is a flight-to-safety situation, in which all currencies decline against the U.S. dollar. The fund is short U.S. dollars, so that would almost certainly cause its net asset value to decline. Fortunately, such situations tend not to be very long-lived, so any decline in the value of the bonds will likely be temporary.

Long-Term Outlook Looks Better

The long-term outlook for emerging market bonds, particularly local currency-denominated bonds such as the ones that comprise the portfolio of the Templeton Emerging Markets Income Fund, is quite good. This comes from the fact that it is very likely that emerging market currencies will rise against the U.S. dollar over the long term.

One of the biggest reasons for this is that the United States will likely have to increase its money supply substantially over the coming years. The Congressional Budget Office projects that the United States will have cumulative deficits of $24 trillion over the next ten years. The agency predicts a $1.9 trillion deficit for fiscal year 2026, which will grow to a $3.1 trillion deficit by 2036. For all ten years, the deficit is projected to exceed the 3.8% of gross domestic product that has been the average over the past five decades. In addition, the agency projects that the debt held by the public will reach 120% by 2036, which exceeds the 106% ratio that existed right at the end of World War 2. Furthermore, unlike the situation at the end of World War 2, the agency does not believe that the situation will get better after 2036. The long-term budget projections, which extend out to 2056, actually show the deficit getting even worse:

(Figures shown are as a percentage of gross domestic product.)

These figures assume that inflation will stay constant at 2% and that there will be no wars nor any changes to current law or entitlement programs that result in increased spending. None of these are realistic assumptions, but even with these assumptions, we can see that the federal deficit grows to 9.1% of U.S. gross domestic product and the public debt-to-GDP ratio increases to 175% by 2056.

It is exceedingly difficult to see any way in which the United States will be able to finance these deficits without the Federal Reserve essentially creating money to purchase debt securities. After all, the majority of the spending growth is due to increasing spending on Social Security and Medicare as the demographic trends in the United States point to the average age of the population increasing over the projection period. This will, over time, result in fewer people paying taxes into these programs and more people collecting from them. The traditional buyers of U.S. debt (namely Japan, China, and the European Union) all have the same demographic trends, and as such, the social programs in those nations will require more funding, leaving them less able to invest in American debt. They may even sell some of their American debt securities to finance their own domestic programs. In the end, this could easily force the Federal Reserve to buy U.S. Treasuries, which increases the money supply and contributes to inflation at home.

Many emerging markets do not have the same demographic concerns as the wealthy developed markets. They have younger populations than the United States or most of Europe. For example, as of 2025, the average age of the population in Egypt is 24.5 years, whereas the average age of the population of the United States surpassed 39 years in 2024. Furthermore, the fertility rate in Egypt is 2.92 children per woman compared to 1.79 in the United States. This points to the social programs in those countries (the ones that have them at least) not having the same problem as the ones in the developed world, as a greater proportion of the population will be working age for longer than in the United States, Japan, or the European Union. This should reduce the need for emerging market governments to run large deficits to support an aging population and support the valuation of their currencies versus the U.S. dollar.

Another factor to consider is that many emerging market nations are commodity exporters, and commodities tend to do fairly well in the face of monetary expansion. For example, the top-performing assets during the 1970s were gold, oil (and energy companies), mining companies, and other commodity producers. It is quite likely that these assets will do well again in an environment in which the Federal Reserve is forced to finance U.S. deficits. Currencies of emerging market nations usually benefit from rising commodity prices, which is at least partly because rising commodity prices tend to improve the balance of trade (exports minus imports) of commodity-producing emerging market nations.

These factors could make local currency emerging market bonds, such as the ones included in the Templeton Emerging Markets Income Fund, good assets to hold over the coming years. Obviously, investors who own this fund would be beneficiaries as this thesis plays out.

Yield Comparison Versus Indices and Peers

The Templeton Emerging Markets Income Fund currently boasts an 8.93% yield at the current share price. This is substantially higher than the yields of any of the major American or global bond indices, including those indices that track emerging market bonds. We can see that quite clearly here:

Index/ETF

Current Yield

Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index (AGG)

3.90%

Bloomberg High Yield Very Liquid Index (JNK)

6.62%

Vanguard Total World Bond ETF (BNDW)

4.13%

Vanguard Total International Bond Index Fund ETF Shares (BNDX)

4.41%

J.P. Morgan EMBI Global Core Index (EMB)

4.99%

J.P. Morgan GBI-EM Global Diversified 15% 4% Floor Index (LEMB)

2.44%

These are certainly not all perfect comparisons for the Templeton Emerging Markets Income Fund. After all, as was shown earlier in this article, TEI invests its assets in bonds that are issued by the governments of emerging market nations and denominated in the currency of the emerging market nation. This is a very different asset class from the securities that comprise some of these indices. For example, AGG and JNK are both comprised only of U.S. dollar-denominated bonds issued by entities that are based in the United States. The two Vanguard index ETFs also include generous allocations to bonds that are issued by entities in developed markets, but the Templeton Emerging Markets Income Fund does not. This leaves the two emerging market bond ETFs—EMB and LEMB. EMB is an index fund that only owns bonds that are issued by emerging market entities that are denominated in U.S. dollars. Unlike TEI, it does not have any emerging market currency exposure. The only one of these exchange-traded index funds that has local currency bonds in its portfolio is LEMB, and as we can see, that index fund has a substantially lower yield than the Templeton Emerging Markets Income Fund.

The clear takeaway here is that investors can earn a substantially larger amount of income by favoring the Templeton Emerging Markets Income Fund over any of the indices. The fund has also delivered a far greater total return over the past five years than any of the above indices:

Over the past five years, the Templeton Emerging Markets Income Fund has delivered a 59.86% total return. The best-performing bond index fund over the same period was the one that tracks the price and yield performance of junk bonds issued by American entities. This index fund managed to deliver a 20.08% total return over the trailing five-year period. Clearly, then, bond investors would have been far better off with the Templeton Emerging Markets Income Fund than with any of the indices.

Closed-end funds are able to produce higher yields through the use of leverage and distribution of capital gains. The Templeton Emerging Markets Income Fund employs both strategies, as do many of its peers. As such, other closed-end funds are much more competitive in terms of yield. We can see this in this peer comparison chart:

Fund Name

Morningstar Classification

Current Yield

Templeton Emerging Markets Income Fund

Fixed Income—Emerging Market Income

8.93%

Morgan Stanley Emerging Markets Debt Fund (MSD)

Fixed Income—Emerging Market Income

7.95%

Virtus Stone Harbor Emerging Markets Income Fund (EDF)

Fixed Income—Emerging Market Income

14.09%

Western Asset Emerging Markets Debt Fund (EMD)

Fixed Income—Emerging Market Income

10.92%

Morgan Stanley Emerging Markets Domestic Debt Fund (EDD)

Fixed Income—Emerging Market Income Fund

9.39%

The median yield out of this group of five funds is the 9.39% of the Morgan Stanley Emerging Markets Debt Fund. Thus, the current yield of the Templeton Emerging Markets Income Fund is lower than the median of the peer group. However, we should keep in mind that not all of these funds have the same composition either. As with the indices, this comes from the fact that emerging markets issue both hard currency bonds (usually denominated in U.S. dollars or euros) and local currency bonds. A fund such as MSD consists almost entirely of U.S. dollar-denominated bonds, whereas EDD and TEI consist of local currency bonds. The thesis that was outlined above is presented with the assumption that investors take on the currency risk, and as such, an emerging markets bond fund that invests primarily in U.S. dollar-denominated bonds may not benefit from the long-term appreciation of emerging market currencies against the U.S. dollar. As such, these funds do not all have the same fundamentals, and investors should look closely at their composition before deciding which one to purchase.

Recent Performance

We last discussed the Templeton Emerging Markets Income Fund on August 14, 2025. The fund has done remarkably well for a bond fund since that date, as its share price has risen by 5.45%, outperforming both American and emerging market bond indices:

If we look at total return, TEI’s performance advantage over the indices has been even more pronounced:

As shown, investors in this fund have benefited from a whopping 22.36% total return since our last discussion on it. Both of the emerging market bond indices only delivered a bit more than 5%. Thus, investors in this fund have likely been quite pleased with its performance over the past several months.

Distribution Analysis

The website for the Templeton Emerging Markets Income Fund states that the fund has the primary objective of providing its investors with a high level of current income. In accordance with this objective, the fund pays a monthly distribution of $0.0475 per share ($0.57 per share annually). This gives the fund an 8.93% yield at the current share price.

The Templeton Emerging Markets Income Fund has released its annual report (linked earlier) for the twelve-month period that ended on December 31, 2025, since our last discussion on it. Let us have a look at this document and see how well it managed to cover its distribution over the full-year 2025 period.

For the twelve-month period that ended on December 31, 2025, the Templeton Emerging Markets Income Fund reported a net investment income of $32,523,217. This was not sufficient to cover the $41,324,866 that the fund distributed over the same period.

Fortunately, the fund was able to make up the difference through capital gains. For the twelve-month period that ended on December 31, 2025, the Templeton Emerging Markets Income Fund reported net realized gains of $3,911,122 along with net unrealized gains of $57,562,814. Overall, the fund’s portfolio provided investment profits of $93,997,153 over the period:

This was sufficient to cover the distributions, but the fund had to rely on its net unrealized gains to do it. This could be concerning, given that unrealized gains are not necessarily permanent. The market can potentially erase unrealized gains in a correction or downturn, so we usually want to be cautious if the fund is relying on them to cover its distributions. Fortunately, though, in this case, there is probably not too much to worry about. The fund’s net investment income plus net realized gains was $36,434,339. That was sufficient to cover all except for $4,890,527 of the distribution. That was less than 10% of the fund’s net unrealized gains, so realistically, the market would have to move against the fund by quite a lot to erase the unrealized gains that it has used to partially offset the distribution. This is rather unlikely to occur, and we probably do not need to worry too much.

Valuation

Shares of the Templeton Emerging Markets Income Fund are currently trading at a 7.24% discount on net asset value. This is a much better price than the 3.36% discount that the shares of this fund have had over the past month.

However, the fund’s current price is actually slightly worse than its long-term average. Over the past five years, shares of this fund have traded at prices ranging from a 17.23% discount on net asset value to a 0.13% premium on net asset value. The average price over the trailing five-year period has been an 8.18% discount on net asset value.

Here is how the current price of the Templeton Emerging Markets Income Fund compares with its peers:

The median price of this group of funds is the 6.82% discount of the Morgan Stanley Emerging Markets Debt Fund, so TEI currently has a price that is slightly better than the peer group median.

Expenses

The fund’s annual report states that it had a 2.38% expense ratio for the full-year 2025 period. As is the case with most closed-end funds, some of these expenses are due to the need to pay interest on the money that the fund has borrowed to obtain leverage. However, the annual report does not provide an expense ratio that excludes interest expenses.

Conclusion

In conclusion, the Templeton Emerging Markets Income Fund is a closed-end fund that offers exposure to local currency emerging market bonds, which is an asset class that many investors do not have much exposure to. This is unfortunate, as these may be some of the best-performing bonds going forward. As such, this fund may be worth watching, and the current price is attractive enough that it may be worth investing in. With that said, though, the conflict in Iran might put selling pressure on some of the bonds in the portfolio for the near term.

data.title: Touchstone International Value Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Fund Highlights

  • Targets value securities, emphasizing stocks of companies based in non-US developed and emerging market countries diversifying its portfolio through exposure to various international economies and sectors
  • Generally, invests in companies that have a market capitalization of $20 million or more at the time of purchase ensuring investment in established companies with reasonable scale and stability
  • Focuses on companies whose securities are currently undervalued in the market but demonstrate signs of recent improvement in business performance or market valuation designed to identify and capitalize on recovering companies before their full value is recognized by the market
  • Uses a sophisticated quantitative model, which analyzes various data points to generate buy or sell signals to minimize emotional bias and use empirical data to guide the buying and selling of securities within the fund's portfolio

Market Recap

International equities delivered resilient gains in the fourth quarter of 2025, underpinned by steady macroeconomic data, improving corporate earnings, and continued signals of broadly accommodative monetary policy. The MSCI ACWI Ex-U.S. Index rose 5.05% while developed international equity markets, as measured by the MSCI EAFE Index, returned 4.86% during the quarter. Emerging markets gained 4.73% while European equities added 6.20% and Pacific markets increased 2.16%. From a style perspective, international value stocks outperformed growth over the quarter, the MSCI ACWI Ex-U.S. Value Index rose 7.61% while the Growth Index advanced 2.56%. From a sector perspective, ten of the eleven broad MSCI EAFE sectors delivered positive returns, with Utilities, Health Care, Materials, and Financials outperforming, while Communication Services was the only sector in negative territory.

While the dollar stabilized in the fourth quarter after its sharp first-half decline, the greenback's full-year depreciation still provided a meaningful tailwind to USD-based returns for international assets in 2025. The Nikkei 225 consolidated near record highs after posting full-year gains of approximately 26%. Strong corporate earnings momentum, ongoing governance reforms, persistent share-buyback activity, and a stable domestic policy backdrop reinforced foreign investor confidence. European markets delivered positive results on the quarter despite being constrained by cautious European Central Bank (ECB) policy, weak manufacturing data, and lingering geopolitical uncertainties.

Germany was a laggard, pressured by sluggish industrial activity and political transition. The UK market generated outsized returns during the quarter, with strength in Financials and Energy.

Emerging market equities delivered solid gains in the fourth quarter, capping an exceptional year that saw the asset class significantly outperform developed market peers. The MSCI Emerging Markets Index posted a 4.73% return for the quarter, driving full-year performance to 33.57%, one of the strongest annual performances in recent history. Key drivers included supportive global liquidity conditions, a weaker US dollar throughout much of 2025, and accommodative policy stances across several major emerging market economies. China's equity market performance turned negative in the fourth quarter, retreating after the explosive rally earlier in the year. Chinese equities declined during the quarter as the initial enthusiasm around September's aggressive stimulus measures faded and investors grew concerned about the durability and scale of policy support. Despite continued rhetoric from authorities about stabilizing growth and supporting strategic sectors, concrete follow-through on fiscal measures disappointed expectations, while underlying economic data, particularly in property and consumer spending, remained weak. Elsewhere in Asia, South Korea emerged as the year's standout performer, with the KOSPI delivering 14.8% on the quarter and one of the strongest global equity performances, rising approximately 76% for the full-year, driven by powerful rallies in semiconductor, defense, and technology stocks tied to global AI demand. Taiwan similarly benefited from semiconductor strength. Latin American markets delivered strong fourth quarter results due to a late-year rally in industrial metals and agricultural commodities, boosting export revenues and fiscal positions across the region. Brazil and Mexico experienced solid gains, benefiting from the combination of stronger commodity prices, a weaker dollar, and central banks' ability to ease policy following successful disinflation.

Portfolio Review

The Touchstone International Value Fund (Class A Shares, Load Waived) outperformed its benchmark, the MSCI ACWI Ex-U.S. Index, for the quarter ended December 31, 2025.

The Fund's deep value bias had a positive impact on relative returns for the quarter as companies that were cheap on the basis of cash flows and earnings, which we favor, outperformed.

Performance attribution further indicates that both stock selection and sector selection added to relative returns. Stock selection relative gains were largely the result of the outperformance of deep value names within Industrials, Financials, and Materials sectors. Within Industrials, holdings in Industrial Conglomerates, Trading Companies & Distributors industries, while avoiding expensive stocks within Aerospace & Defense industry added to returns.

Within Financials, holdings in Diversified Banks, Life & Health Insurance, and Asset Management & Custody Banks industries added to returns. Within Materials, holdings in Gold, Steel, and Aluminum industry stocks added to returns. On the negative side, stock selection detracted within the Consumer Staples and Utilities sectors. From a sector perspective, relative gains were largely the result of our overweight to Financials and Materials coupled with underweight positioning to the Communication Services sector.

Over the quarter, performance at the individual stock level reflected the Fund's systematic emphasis on valuation and fundamentals rather than any idiosyncratic stock-specific views. Contributors tended to be companies that were attractively valued and exhibited solid fundamentals, strong cash flow generation, improving earnings trends, and positive sentiment and momentum signals. These positions benefited as the market recognized improving fundamentals or as previously discounted stocks mean-reverted toward fair value. In the case of SK hynix Inc. and Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company Ltd. (TSM), we did not own these stocks in the Fund as they are currently expensive, and we were rewarded as valuations compressed during the quarter. The United Laboratories International Holdings Ltd. (ULIHF) position (Health Care sector) faced some recent headwinds relating to the Hong Kong pharmaceutical sector which has faced recent headwinds, including concerns over regulatory hurdles in China and potential downward pressure on drug pricing. This has led to a cautious sentiment among investors.

At quarter end, the Fund (vs. the benchmark index) was overweight Financials, Materials, and Health Care sectors while underweight Communication Services, Information Technology, and Industrials sectors. At the industry level, the Fund was overweight Pharmaceuticals, Insurance, and Machinery, while underweight Semiconductor & Semiconductor Equipment, Aerospace & Defense, and Electrical Equipment. Changes in our positioning are consistent with our systematic, valuation-driven approach, which is generally reflected in our emphasis on companies that were attractively valued and fundamentally sound—businesses generating solid cash flow and earnings, with supportive sentiment or momentum characteristics.

Outlook and Conclusion

As a systematic, valuation-driven manager, our process does not rely on macroeconomic forecasts or top-down positioning. We maintain a disciplined, bottom-up approach focused on identifying attractively valued and fundamentally sound companies within a tightly risk-controlled framework. Country, sector, and industry exposures remain close to benchmark, so the Fund positioning reflects where we find the best opportunities at the stock level rather than explicit macro views. While we can offer little in the way of near-term guidance, we remain optimistic that the extremely attractive deep value positioning of the Fund will be rewarded over time. At quarter end, the Fund is trading at significant valuation discounts relative to the MSCI ACWI Ex-U.S. Index. The Fund is trading at 10.8x forward earnings compared to 16.6x for the benchmark, 1.4x book value versus 2.2x for the benchmark, and 6.7x cash flow compared to 12x for the benchmark. In addition, the portfolio is yielding 3.84% compared to 2.56% for the MSCI ACWI Ex-U.S. Index.
